title: Policy Normalization
summary: The Federal Reserve announced on October 29, 2025, that it would cease balance sheet runoff starting December 1, 2025, concluding a process that began in June 2022. Since then, Fed securities holdings declined by more than $2.2 trillion, reducing holdings as a share of nominal GDP from 33% to 20%. The Committee judged reserves were approaching ample levels, making the cessation of runoff appropriate.
key_points: FOMC announced cessation of securities runoff effective December 1, 2025, rolling over all Treasury principal payments and reinvesting agency MBS principal into Treasury bills
Since June 2022, total Fed securities holdings declined by more than $2.2 trillion, including $1.6 trillion in Treasuries and $600 billion in agency MBS
Fed securities as share of nominal GDP fell 13 percentage points, from 33% to 20%
Original runoff caps were $30B/month for Treasuries (rising to $60B after 3 months) and $17.5B/month for agency MBS (rising to $35B after 3 months)
Committee will maintain securities holdings in amounts needed for the ample reserves regime
The decision was driven by money market conditions suggesting reserve levels were approaching ample levels
why_it_matters: The conclusion of quantitative tightening represents a significant shift in Fed policy after 3.5 years of balance sheet reduction. This affects bank liquidity conditions, money market dynamics, and the overall supply of reserves in the financial system. The shift to reinvestment mode signals the Fed views financial conditions as stabilized and reserve levels as sufficiently abundant.
macro_context: This policy change occurs after an extended period of QT that removed substantial liquidity from the financial system. The cessation of runoff means the Fed will no longer be a net seller of Treasuries and MBS, which could affect Treasury bill supply dynamics and money market conditions.
market_structure_context: The transition from active balance sheet reduction to reinvestment represents a structural shift in how the Fed operates in secondary markets. With the ample reserves framework, the Fed will manage securities holdings as needed to maintain reserves without active QT.
notes: Source is authoritative Federal Reserve official documentation. Contains substantive policy details and specific numbers. Historical context from 2022-2025 QT program included. Document captures the conclusion of a major monetary policy normalization cycle.
